planting.note: Root stem cuttings easily in moist potting mix or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, organically rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade after last frost; Brazilian plume flower blooms most heavily in late summer to fall and again in spring in frost-free climates.

directions: Root stem cuttings in moist potting mix or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, organically rich soil in partial to full shade; Brazilian plume flower is a South American native soft-wooded shrub that delivers one of the most spectacular flower displays possible in a shaded landscape — dense, upright, plume-like clusters of tubular rose-pink to deep magenta flowers from late summer through frost, and again in spring. The large, oval, dark-green leaves are handsome throughout the year. Hummingbirds visit the flowers repeatedly. Requires consistent moisture and resents drought; water deeply during hot, dry spells. Cut back by one-half to two-thirds in early spring to promote vigorous, floriferous new growth. In zone 9 it may be killed to the ground by hard frost but re-sprouts from the base; mulch heavily in winter.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost or root stem cuttings in moist perlite; plant out after last frost in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade. Pinch tips regularly to maintain compact, bushy form.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost in moist seed-starting mix in a warm, brightly lit location; polka dot plant is a Madagascar native grown for its vivid, spotted foliage — broad, dark-green leaves thickly splashed with pink, white, red, or magenta spots and blotches that give the plant an almost unnaturally colorful appearance, making it one of the most striking foliage plants for shaded containers and garden beds. Pinch growing tips regularly to maintain compact, bushy growth and prevent leggy, flopping stems. Plants flower with small lavender spikes in late season; remove flowers to maintain foliage quality. Overwinters as a tender perennial in zones 10–12; grown as an annual in cooler climates. Excellent in combination with ferns, hostas, and caladiums in shaded container plantings.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions 2–3 inches deep in moist to wet, organically rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun after last frost; calla lily tolerates standing water seasonally and performs best with regular moisture throughout the growing season.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions 2–3 inches deep in moist to wet, organically rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun after last frost; calla lily is a South African native that produces some of the most architecturally perfect flowers in horticulture — large, flaring, pure-white spathe bracts surrounding a golden yellow spadix — on tall, succulent stems above glossy, broadly arrow-shaped, dark-green leaves. Plants bloom in spring and early summer in mild climates, tolerating seasonal wet feet and even standing water. In hot-summer climates they prefer afternoon shade; in cool coastal areas they thrive in full sun. After bloom, reduce water and allow foliage to yellow and die back. The colored-flowered Zantedeschia hybrids (yellow, pink, purple) are less hardy, requiring much drier summer dormancy conditions than the common white species.

planting.note: Plant tubers 3–4 inches deep in fall in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Italian arum is winter-active and produces foliage through the cold months, filling the off-season gap when most shade plants are dormant.

directions: Plant tubers in fall in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Italian arum has an unusual reverse-season growth cycle — the large, glossy, dark-green, arrow-shaped leaves emerge in fall, display through winter with striking silver or gray-white marbling, and die back by early summer, then clusters of brilliant orange-red berries on bare stems persist through summer, providing the most visually arresting fruit display of any shade garden perennial. The unusual winter-foliage-summer-berry cycle makes it an invaluable complement to spring- and summer-growing companions. 'Marmoratum' has particularly vivid silver marbling. All plant parts are toxic — the berries are especially dangerous. Naturalizes slowly by self-seeding to form colonies in the deep, undisturbed shade of mature trees where few other plants perform.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens or divisions in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in spring; heucherella is sterile and does not set seed — it propagates only by division and is sold exclusively as named clones.

directions: Plant container specimens or divisions in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; heucherella is an intergeneric hybrid between Heuchera (alumroot) and Tiarella (foamflower) that combines the bold, colorful foliage of heuchera with the running, spreading habit and delicate flowers of tiarella. The result is a versatile, shade-tolerant perennial with large, deeply lobed, often intricately patterned or color-contrasted leaves — available in deep burgundy, caramel, chartreuse, and multi-tone combinations — and frothy white to pink flower spikes in spring. The sterile hybrid is generally more heat-tolerant and vigorous than tiarella and holds its ornamental foliage better through summer than many heuchera. Excellent as a shade groundcover, border edger, and container plant. 'Sweet Tea', 'Bridget Bloom', 'Stoplight', and 'Solar Eclipse' are outstanding named selections.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost — seeds are extremely fine and should be pressed onto the soil surface without covering; transplant after last frost in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade.

directions: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost, pressing the dust-fine seed onto moist surface-sown seed mix without covering — seeds need light to germinate; grow on in a bright window until after last frost, then transplant to moist, slightly acidic soil in partial shade for best summer longevity. Edging lobelia is a South African native producing masses of small, two-lipped flowers in intense sky-blue, violet, white, or magenta-rose in a continuous cascade from spring through early summer, then again in fall after summer heat abates and it is typically sheared back. The plant blooms most prolifically in cool weather and fades in peak summer heat; afternoon shade significantly extends its performance in hot summer climates. Popular for container edges, hanging baskets, and formal bed borders. 'Crystal Palace' (deep blue, bronze foliage), 'Cobalt Blue', and 'White Lady' are classic selections.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center
North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
